reasoning: This is an exceptionally well-structured plan with clean architectural boundaries. Each top-level task owns a single architectural layer (Task 1: infrastructure, Task 2: catalog data+API, Task 3: RMS data+API, etc.), and subtasks further decompose into single-concern units — migrations are separated from business logic, shared middleware from service-specific routes, Dockerfiles from K8s manifests. The dependency DAG is infrastructure-first (Task 1 → services 2-6 → Morgan 7 → frontends 8-9 → hardening 10) with good parallelism among services. Decision points are thorough with well-researched options and impact analysis, correctly identifying architectural ambiguities (availability model, Stripe integration approach, Valkey HA). The only structural concern is Task 2's shared crate (subtasks 2001-2005) being consumed by Tasks 4 and 5 without an explicit cross-task dependency declaration — the shared crate initialization lives inside Task 2 but is architecturally a foundation for all Rust services.
suggestions: Extract the shared Rust crate initialization (subtasks 2001-2005: DB pool, health checks, metrics, rate limiting, API key auth) into its own top-level task or make Tasks 4 and 5 explicitly depend on Task 2's shared crate subtasks, since currently Tasks 4 and 5 only depend on Task 1 but silently require the shared crate from Task 2 — this is a hidden structural dependency that could cause parallel execution failures.

Task 10 (Production Hardening) subtask 10004 creates a default-deny NetworkPolicy that will immediately break all running services if applied before the allow policies (10005-10008) — add explicit dependency ordering so 10005-10008 are applied atomically before or simultaneously with 10004, or restructure as a single subtask that applies the full policy set together to prevent a deployment gap.

Task 7 (Morgan AI Agent) spans multiple architectural concerns: agent configuration, MCP tool server, 8+ skill implementations, 3 channel adapters (Signal, voice, WebSocket), K8s manifests, and monitoring — consider splitting into separate tasks by boundary (e.g., 'Morgan Core Agent + MCP Tools', 'Morgan Channel Adapters', 'Morgan Skills') to improve single-agent implementability and reduce the coordination surface within one task.

reasoning: Task sizing is uneven: several top-level tasks (e.g., 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 10) each bundle many days/weeks of work, even though some have good subtasks; this will stall execution and make incremental delivery fragile. Dependency ordering is mostly infrastructure-first, but some subtasks incorrectly gate work (e.g., catalog K8s manifests depend on Dockerfile 2014<-2013<-2012 even though manifests can be written earlier) and some dependencies are missing or inconsistent (e.g., Finance 4001 references shared crate as ../common vs task 2 uses shared; also 4014 secret name mismatch). Decision points are generally captured with real options, but some critical cross-cutting decisions (auth model/token formats, service URL/env var contracts, GDPR endpoint consistency) are left implicit and will cause integration churn.
suggestions: Split the monolithic top-level services (Catalog/RMS/Finance/Vetting/Social/Morgan/Frontend/Mobile/Prod Hardening) into clearer 1–3 day deliverables that ship working increments early (e.g., for Catalog: API skeleton + health/metrics + 1 read-only endpoint + k8s deploy as first milestone; then CRUD; then availability/checkout; then OpenAPI; then GDPR). Make each increment deployable/testable in-cluster.

Fix avoidable serial chains and enable parallelism by decoupling artifacts: allow writing K8s manifests in parallel with service implementation and Dockerfiles (e.g., Catalog 2014 should depend on only 'service port/paths + env contract' not on 2013; similarly many hardening netpol/RBAC/dashboard tasks in 10 can start once namespaces/labels exist, not after every app is finished). Add explicit 'API contract freeze' tasks early to unblock frontend/mobile/Morgan tools.

Standardize and add decision points/acceptance criteria for cross-service contracts: (a) consistent env var names (POSTGRES_URL vs DATABASE_URL; R2 keys; secret names), (b) consistent auth header and API key layout across Rust/Go/Node, (c) consistent GDPR endpoints (path + method + request body) across services and the orchestrator (task 10014 currently calls /api/gdpr/delete while services define /api/v1/gdpr/customer/:id DELETE). Update tasks to enforce these contracts and add an integration smoke-test task that verifies all services + Morgan tools against those contracts.

reasoning: This is a genuinely large, complex distributed system and the task count (10 top-level tasks, ~130 subtasks) is proportional to the scope — a full-stack platform with 5 microservices, an AI agent, web frontend, mobile app, and production hardening. Task decomposition is tight: each subtask has a clear single concern (e.g., 1002 deploys the PG cluster, 1003 runs init SQL, 1004 deploys PgBouncer — properly separated). Dependency ordering is valid with no forward references and good parallelism signals, though some tasks are overly chained where parallel execution was possible (e.g., 4003/4004 both depend on 4002 but not on each other, correctly marked, but 4013 waits on 4011 which seems unnecessary). Decision points are excellent — the PRD's genuine ambiguities (LinkedIn strategy, credit provider, availability model, scoring weights) are all surfaced with concrete options and impact analysis. Test strategies are specific and measurable with both happy path and error cases consistently covered.
suggestions: Tasks 8012 (accessibility audit) and 8013 (performance optimization) are tester/implementer tasks that depend on nearly every other frontend task — consider making them a single final frontend hardening task since they always run together and splitting them adds coordination overhead without enabling any parallelism. The accessibility fixes found in 8012 will immediately require implementation changes that 8013 also touches.

Task 7012 (tool call parallelization and performance validation) is listed as a 'tester' subagentType but contains implementation work (configuring OpenClaw parallel dispatch, fan-out/fan-in patterns) — split the implementation of parallel tool calling into an earlier subtask under 7002 or 7003 where it belongs, and keep 7012 as a pure performance validation/benchmark task.
